front: Work: structured after Euclid's Elements with axioms, definitions, and propositions; argues God is the only substance identical with Nature ('Deus sive Natura')
Concept: the distinction between the active, productive aspect of nature identical to God, and the passive aspect sustained by it — two Latin terms for 'nature naturing' and 'nature natured'
Concept: 'each thing strives to preserve its own being' — the doctrine of existential inertia that drives all human passions, appearing as Proposition IIIP6
Work: section 'Of Human Bondage' examines how the affects control human life; final section praises knowledge of the third kind as coming from God
Work: argues Moses did not write the Torah, denies miracles, and advocates religious toleration and free speech; Steven Nadler called it 'A Book Forged in Hell'
Philosopher: the 1780s Pantheism Controversy between Jacobi and Mendelssohn erupted over Lessing's deathbed confession of following this thinker

Philosopher: Gilles Deleuze called this thinker the 'prince of philosophers' and wrote Expressionism in Philosophy about him; placed between Duns Scotus and Nietzsche as theorizers of the univocity of being
Philosopher: Antonio Negri's The Savage Anomaly and Hardt and Negri's Multitude draw on this thinker's political concept of the 'multitude'

Philosopher: argued that two rational men can form a composite being twice as powerful as either; equated 'right' with 'power' and argued the sovereign has limited power since everyone is 'the master of his own thoughts'
Philosopher: the only book published under this thinker's own name was a critique of Descartes' Principles of Philosophy, done 'in geometrical order'
